name: scripting
context: Write robust shell scripts with variables, conditionals, loops, functions, arrays, error handling, and traps
info: Shell scripting turns one-off commands into repeatable, automated workflows. This context covers the complete scripting toolkit: shebang lines and portability, variables and arrays, conditionals (if/elif/else, case), loops (for, while, until), functions and return values, argument parsing ($1, $@, getopts), error handling (set -euo pipefail, trap), here-documents for templating, and best practices for robust scripts. Agents generate and execute shell scripts for build automation, deployment, data processing, environment setup, and system administration. See [[shell/bash]] for command syntax, [[shell/environment]] for configuration, [[shell/pipes]] for data flow, and [[shell/processes]] for process control within scripts.
body:
# Shell Scripting

Shell scripts automate sequences of commands. A well-written script is repeatable, handles errors gracefully, and clearly communicates its intent.

## Script Structure

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Description: What this script does
# Usage: ./script.sh [options] <arguments>

# ─── Configuration ──────────────────────
READONLY_VAR="immutable"
DEFAULT_PORT="${PORT:-8080}"

# ─── Functions ──────────────────────────
usage() {
  echo "Usage: $0 [-v] [-p port] <name>" >&2
  exit 1
}

log() {
  echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] $*" >&2
}

cleanup() {
  log "Cleaning up..."
  rm -f "$tmpfile"
}

# ─── Main ───────────────────────────────
trap cleanup EXIT

tmpfile=$(mktemp)
log "Starting..."

# ... script logic ...

log "Done."
```

## Shebang

The first line tells the kernel which interpreter to use:

```bash
#!/bin/bash          # Bash (most common)
#!/bin/sh            # POSIX sh (portable)
#!/usr/bin/env bash  # Find bash in PATH (portable across systems)
#!/usr/bin/env python3  # Python scripts
```

Always use `#!/usr/bin/env bash` for portability — Bash may live at `/bin/bash`, `/usr/bin/bash`, or `/usr/local/bin/bash` depending on the system.

Make scripts executable: `chmod +x script.sh`

## Variables

```bash
# Assignment (no spaces around =)
name="world"
count=0

# Readonly
readonly VERSION="1.0.0"
declare -r CONFIG_DIR="/etc/myapp"

# Local (inside functions)
my_func() {
  local result="computed value"
  echo "$result"
}

# Default values
port="${PORT:-8080}"       # Use 8080 if PORT is unset or empty
host="${HOST:=localhost}"  # Same, but also assigns

# Positional parameters
# $0 — script name
# $1, $2, ... — arguments
# $# — number of arguments
# $@ — all arguments (individually quoted when in "$@")
# $* — all arguments as single string

echo "Script: $0"
echo "First arg: $1"
echo "All args: $@"
echo "Arg count: $#"
```

## Arrays

```bash
# Indexed array
fruits=("apple" "banana" "cherry")
echo "${fruits[0]}"          # apple
echo "${fruits[@]}"          # All elements
echo "${#fruits[@]}"         # Length: 3

# Append
fruits+=("date")

# Iterate
for fruit in "${fruits[@]}"; do
  echo "$fruit"
done

# Associative array (Bash 4+)
declare -A config
config[host]="localhost"
config[port]="8080"
echo "${config[host]}:${config[port]}"

# Iterate associative array
for key in "${!config[@]}"; do
  echo "$key=${config[$key]}"
done

# Array from command output
files=($(find . -name '*.sh' -type f))
# Safer (handles spaces):
mapfile -t files < <(find . -name '*.sh' -type f)
```

## Conditionals

```bash
# if/elif/else
if [[ -f "$config_file" ]]; then
  source "$config_file"
elif [[ -f "$default_config" ]]; then
  source "$default_config"
else
  echo "No config found" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# Inline conditional
[[ -d "$dir" ]] || mkdir -p "$dir"
[[ -f "$file" ]] && rm "$file"

# case statement (pattern matching)
case "$1" in
  start)
    start_server
    ;;
  stop)
    stop_server
    ;;
  restart)
    stop_server
    start_server
    ;;
  -h|--help)
    usage
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Unknown command: $1" >&2
    usage
    ;;
esac
```

## Loops

```bash
# for — iterate over list
for file in *.txt; do
  echo "Processing: $file"
done

# for — iterate over array
for item in "${array[@]}"; do
  process "$item"
done

# for — C-style
for (( i=0; i<10; i++ )); do
  echo "$i"
done

# while — condition loop
count=0
while (( count < 5 )); do
  echo "$count"
  (( count++ ))
done

# while — read lines from file
while IFS= read -r line; do
  echo "Line: $line"
done < input.txt

# while — read lines from command
while IFS= read -r file; do
  echo "Found: $file"
done < <(find . -name '*.log')

# until — loop until condition is true
until curl -sf http://localhost:8080/health; do
  echo "Waiting for server..." >&2
  sleep 1
done

# Infinite loop with break
while true; do
  read -r -p "Continue? [y/n] " answer
  case "$answer" in
    [Yy]) ;;
    [Nn]) break ;;
  esac
done
```

## Functions

```bash
# Define function
greet() {
  local name="$1"
  echo "Hello, $name!"
}

# Call function
greet "World"

# Return values (exit codes only, 0-255)
check_file() {
  [[ -f "$1" ]]   # Returns 0 (true) or 1 (false)
}

if check_file "config.json"; then
  echo "Config exists"
fi

# Return data via stdout
get_timestamp() {
  date '+%Y%m%d-%H%M%S'
}
ts=$(get_timestamp)

# Return data via global variable
result=""
compute() {
  result=$(( $1 * 2 ))
}
compute 21
echo "$result"   # 42
```

## Argument Parsing

```bash
# Simple positional arguments
if [[ $# -lt 2 ]]; then
  echo "Usage: $0 <source> <dest>" >&2
  exit 1
fi
source_dir="$1"
dest_dir="$2"

# getopts — short options
verbose=false
port=8080
while getopts ':vp:h' opt; do
  case "$opt" in
    v) verbose=true ;;
    p) port="$OPTARG" ;;
    h) usage ;;
    :) echo "Option -$OPTARG requires an argument" >&2; exit 1 ;;
    ?) echo "Unknown option -$OPTARG" >&2; exit 1 ;;
  esac
done
shift $((OPTIND - 1))  # Remove parsed options, leaving positional args

# Manual long option parsing
while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
  case "$1" in
    --verbose) verbose=true; shift ;;
    --port) port="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    --port=*) port="${1#*=}"; shift ;;
    --help) usage ;;
    --) shift; break ;;
    -*) echo "Unknown option: $1" >&2; exit 1 ;;
    *) break ;;
  esac
done
```

## Error Handling

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# set -e: Exit on any command failure
# set -u: Error on undefined variables
# set -o pipefail: Pipe fails if any command fails

# trap — run cleanup on exit/error
tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
trap 'rm -rf "$tmpdir"' EXIT  # Always clean up, even on error

trap 'echo "Error on line $LINENO" >&2' ERR  # Log errors

# Explicit error handling (when set -e is too aggressive)
if ! result=$(some_command 2>&1); then
  echo "Command failed: $result" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# OR operator for recovery
cd /some/path || { echo "Directory not found" >&2; exit 1; }

# Check prerequisites
for cmd in git node npm; do
  command -v "$cmd" &>/dev/null || {
    echo "Required command not found: $cmd" >&2
    exit 1
  }
done

# Retry pattern
retry() {
  local max_attempts="$1"; shift
  local attempt=1
  while (( attempt <= max_attempts )); do
    if "$@"; then
      return 0
    fi
    echo "Attempt $attempt/$max_attempts failed" >&2
    (( attempt++ ))
    sleep 1
  done
  return 1
}
retry 3 curl -sf http://localhost:8080/health
```

## Best Practices

1. **Always start with `set -euo pipefail`** — catches undefined variables, failed commands, and pipe failures
2. **Quote all variable expansions** — `"$var"`, `"$@"`, `"$(command)"` — prevents word splitting bugs
3. **Use `[[ ]]` instead of `[ ]`** — safer, supports `&&`, `||`, `=~`, no word splitting
4. **Use `local` in functions** — prevents variable leaks to calling scope
5. **Use `readonly` for constants** — prevents accidental reassignment
6. **Use `trap cleanup EXIT`** — ensures cleanup runs even on error
7. **Check prerequisites early** — verify required commands, files, and variables before doing work
8. **Write to stderr for diagnostics** — `echo "info" >&2` keeps stdout clean for data
9. **Use `mapfile`/`readarray` for array building** — safer than `arr=($(command))`
10. **Lint with shellcheck** — `shellcheck script.sh` catches common errors statically

sections.title: Error handling and set options
sections.body: `set -e` exits on any command failure. `set -u` errors on undefined variables. `set -o pipefail` makes pipes fail if any command fails. Combine as `set -euo pipefail` at script top. `trap 'cleanup' EXIT` runs cleanup on script exit (success or failure). `trap 'echo error' ERR` catches errors. `$LINENO` gives the line number where error occurred. Use `command || { handle; }` for inline error handling. Sometimes `set -e` is too strict — disable with `set +e` for sections that may legitimately fail.

sections.title: Argument parsing
sections.body: `$1` through `$9` are positional parameters. `$#` is the count. `$@` is all arguments (quote as `"$@"`). `$0` is the script name. `shift` removes the first argument. `getopts` parses short options (`-v`, `-p value`). For long options (`--verbose`, `--port=8080`), use a manual while/case loop. Always validate argument count early and provide a usage function. `$OPTIND` tracks getopts position — `shift $((OPTIND-1))` after getopts to access remaining positional args.

sections.title: Functions and return values
sections.body: Define with `name() { body; }`. `local` makes variables function-scoped. Functions return exit codes (0-255) via `return`. Return data via stdout: `result=$(my_func)`. Functions share the global scope unless `local` is used — always use `local` for temporary variables. Functions can be exported to subshells with `export -f func_name`. Recursive functions work but watch for stack depth. Use `declare -f func_name` to print a function's definition.

sections.title: Loops and iteration
sections.body: `for x in list; do` iterates over a list. `for (( i=0; i<n; i++ )); do` is C-style. `while condition; do` loops while true. `until condition; do` loops until true. `while IFS= read -r line; do ... done < file` reads lines safely. `break` exits a loop. `continue` skips to next iteration. `break 2` breaks out of nested loops. Use `< <(command)` instead of `command | while` to avoid subshell variable scope issues.

sections.title: Arrays
sections.body: Indexed arrays: `arr=(a b c)`, access `${arr[0]}`, all elements `"${arr[@]}"`, length `${#arr[@]}`. Append: `arr+=(d)`. Delete: `unset 'arr[1]'`. Associative arrays (Bash 4+): `declare -A map`, set `map[key]=val`, keys `"${!map[@]}"`. Build array from command: `mapfile -t arr < <(command)` (safer than `arr=($(command))` which breaks on spaces). Iterate: `for x in "${arr[@]}"`. Slice: `"${arr[@]:start:count}"`.

sections.title: Linting with ShellCheck
sections.body: ShellCheck is a static analysis tool for shell scripts. Install: `apt install shellcheck` or `brew install shellcheck`. Run: `shellcheck script.sh`. It catches common bugs: unquoted variables, useless cat, deprecated syntax, POSIX incompatibilities, unreachable code. Integrates with most editors and CI. Disable specific warnings with `# shellcheck disable=SC2034`. Online version: shellcheck.net. Every agent-generated script should pass shellcheck before execution.
